note: Public display only: no roles, employers, or residence details. Each entry has `quotes` — short paraphrased lines aligned with the topic/subtopic where that pin is placed on the globe. `themes` are optional search tags.

quotes: People compare what subsidized or income-restricted options actually exist versus what gets built market-rate.
A lot of threads argue the math: rent versus owning, and whether moving farther out still saves money.
Bus connections come up as the fragile link between affordable pockets and everything else.
Mixing bus, train, and sometimes a water crossing is treated as normal — until one leg slips and the whole trip balloons.
Riders describe the multi-leg commute as worth it when one segment feels reliable and pleasant.
The conversation keeps returning to small delays stacking: one late transfer reshapes the whole evening.
Posts argue who should pay for the system — riders, drivers through pricing, or broader tax bases.
Congestion and roadway pricing get framed as either equity fixes or regressive squeezes, depending who is posting.
A common thread: distrust that revenue will come back as service rather than filling other budget holes.
Commuter rail and subway threads overlap when people compare which line is the least bad backup.
Service alerts and shuttle buses show up as the default vocabulary of a bad week.
Route-planning posts treat transfers as puzzles: minimize walking, minimize waiting, minimize surprise.
Headways dominate: when trains are hourly, the whole region’s schedule bends around a single clock.
People swap tactics for making a long rail day tolerable — where to sit, when to bail to a bus.
Parking at the station is a recurring subplot: cost, availability, and whether driving the first mile is worth it.
Equity threads ask who gets skipped when service is thin evenings and Sundays.
Riders describe forced tradeoffs: extra fare to a ride-hail just to reach the first train.
The same gap gets narrated as a budgeting problem and as a workforce-access problem.
Late-night and very early trains are treated like oxygen for shift work — when they vanish, plans collapse.
Schedules written for nine-to-five riders leave everyone else doing improvised choreography.
People ask for headways they can memorize instead of apps they have to babysit.
Fare-free and reduced-fare ideas show up next to skepticism about who would pay instead.
Free-transit advocates argue ridership and fairness; critics argue cost and crowding.
Student passes and bulk discounts get compared to what peer cities try.
Transfer design is a personality in these threads: stairs, elevators, weather, and whether you miss the window.
People compare apps, paper schedules, and gut feeling — whichever failed them last.
Early trains and late trains are discussed as different systems entirely.
Red Line reliability is a meme and a trauma dump at the same time.
Commuter rail truncations send people hunting for bus bridges they did not plan on.
Dwell time and platform crowding get blamed in the same breath as signal problems.
Monthly passes and fare capping show up as mental accounting: when does a pass actually pay off?
People compare CharlieCard, phone wallets, and whatever the vending machine ate last week.
Small friction — a dead reader, a wrong fare gate — gets magnified on a tight connection.
TOD threads ask whether new density actually produces more service or just more riders competing for the same seats.
Frequency upgrades are read as promises; missed targets are read as broken contracts.
Station access by foot and bus gets debated as part of the housing package, not an afterthought.
Airport and Logan-adjacent routing is a magnet for “is the T ever sensible for a flight?” posts.
People compare Silver Line, Blue Line, and just eating the taxi cost.
Luggage, weather, and late arrivals turn into a secondary puzzle on top of fares.
Community calendars and “what’s on this weekend” energy collides with practical questions about venues.
Cheap or free public events get sorted by transit reachability as much as by interest.
Students and newcomers ask for low-stakes ways to learn the city without spending a fortune.
Tap-to-pay and phone-wallet bugs are a whole genre: it worked yesterday, it fails at the gate today.
People compare CharlieCard to systems in other cities they’ve visited.
Fare enforcement fairness gets argued alongside technology rollout speed.
Service cuts linger in memory longer than the press release: riders track which routes never returned.
Outrage posts mix politics, union news, and personal lateness into one emotional packet.
Skepticism defaults to “show me the rider impact,” not the agency press line.
State funding threads map who controls the purse strings versus who feels the crowding on platforms.
Regional rail ambitions bump into legislative pacing and bond politics.
Commenters argue whether new revenue should buy maintenance, expansion, or both at half quality.
Blocked bike lanes are documented like crime scenes: photos, maps, and repeat offenders.
Cyclists and drivers talk past each other about enforcement versus design.
Separated lanes show up as the rare thing both sides sometimes agree could reduce conflict.
